Search self-heating rice on Hema. (0.80) 5. Select the cheapest self-heating rice and add it to the cart. (1.00) contact, deleting user data, or performing an unauthorized financial transaction. Triggering any veto condition assigns the trajectory an automatic score of 0. For Task 1 in Table 3, the veto condition is triggered if the agent coins the video without explicit authorization or operates on non-target video. Conditional branching. Real-world GUI tasks often admit multiple valid execution paths depending on the agents environment or account state. To accommodate this, we design sub-goals with conditional branches, allowing the agent to attain full score through any valid path. In the final sub-goal of Task 1 in Table 3, for example, the agent receives full credit either for coining the video when coins are available or for confirming their unavailability when they are not, so that it is not penalized for factors beyond its control.

sections.title: 5.3.2 Executable Verification Rules
sections.content: Existing evaluation strategies trade off robustness against scalability, with pure XPath matching being brittle to UI changes and programmatic state-checking requiring application-specific code. To balance these concerns, we propose dual verification framework that combines XML structure matching with logical semantic rules, enabling both fine-grained sub-goal verification and flexible evaluation of semantically equivalent actions. XML structure matching. This component validates whether the expected UI elements are present or have been acted upon by evaluating XPath expressions over the UI hierarchy. For each sub-goal, we encode the expected element conditions as an XPath query. For instance, to verify that the agent has searched for high-speed train from Beijing to Shanghai for the next day, we evaluate: //*[contains(@package, 'ctrip') and contains(@text, 'Beijing') and contains(@resource-id, 'depart_station')] and //*[contains(@text, 'Tomorrow')] and //*[contains(@text, 'Shanghai') and contains(@resource-id, 'arrive_station')] This query is satisfied only when the departure city, arrival city, and travel date all appear together on the Ctrip page. To verify instead whether the agent clicked the Add to Cart button, we evaluate: 20 (a) Task 2: playlist transfer. (b) Task 3: multi-application planning. Figure 8 Verification pipeline on two representative tasks from Table 3. //*[(contains(@content-desc, 'add to cart') or contains(@text, 'add to cart')) and bbox_contains_point(../@bounds, $point)] Here the matched element must carry the add to cart label and contain the agents click point within its bounding box, confirming that the agent acted on the correct control. XML matching relies on the UI hierarchy, which does not always expose all on-screen text, as some elements lack text attributes and others render text as images. We therefore complement it with optical character recognition (OCR) over the screenshots to recover these text-based conditions. Logical semantic rules. While XML structure matching verifies individual UI elements and the interactions on them, it cannot capture higher-level constraints that require reasoning across multiple steps or applications. To address this, we introduce logical semantic rules that validate two such constraint types, namely sequential constraints and consistency constraints. Sequential constraints ensure that operations occur in the correct order. In Task 3 of Table 3, for instance, the agent must identify the high-speed train and its departure station before querying the travel time to that station via Amap, since the query is only well defined once the station is known. Consistency constraints instead verify that information is preserved and propagated across steps. In Task 2 of Table 3, the three songs added in QQ Music must match those in the Xiaohongshu playlist, which we check by comparing the song titles entered in QQ Musics search box against those collected from the Xiaohongshu page via OCR. Each constraint is implemented as code function that the framework invokes during evaluation, enabling automated verification of task requirements that go beyond per-step element matching. Figure 8 shows how the two mechanisms work together on two tasks from Table 3. Figure 8(a) combines XML matching with consistency constraint to validate the cross-application information flow in Task 2, while Figure 8(b) applies XML matching to verify the sub-goals of Task 3 across Ctrip, Amap, and Hema.

sections.title: 5.3.3 Auto-Eval Pipeline
sections.content: The verification rules are applied by an automated pipeline that takes task and GUI trajectory, namely sequence of screenshots, XML documents, and actions, and produces score in four stages. It first runs OCR over each screenshot to recover text that the XML hierarchy omits. It then checks the veto conditions against the recovered text and the XML hierarchy, terminating with score of 0 whenever one is triggered. Otherwise it evaluates each sub-goal by matching the pre-defined XPath queries over the UI hierarchy and invoking the code functions that validate the sequential and consistency constraints. Finally it computes the score as the fraction of completed sub-goals.

sections.title: 6.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Xiaomi-GUI-0 adopts Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct [5] as its backbone. The three training stages are applied sequentially, with each stage initialized from the checkpoint of the preceding one: SFT starts from the Instruct version, Step RL from the SFT checkpoint, and Agentic RL from the Step RL checkpoint. Hardware and frameworks. All experiments are conducted on 64 NVIDIA H100 GPUs, organized as 8 nodes with 8 GPUs each. The training pipeline is built on verl [31] as the reinforcement learning framework, with Megatron-Core [34] as the training backend and SGLang [57] as the rollout engine. Training data. The SFT stage uses approximately 1.2 million GUI step-level samples drawn from around 120 thousand trajectories collected on both real devices and Android emulators, together with 4.4 million grounding samples for fine-grained screen understanding. The grounding samples require the model to parse all visible elements on the screen and predict their coordinates, functions, textual content, and other attributes. The Step RL stage further introduces approximately 0.4 million GUI step-level samples drawn from around 40 thousand trajectories. The Agentic RL stage operates on several thousand tasks, whose trajectories are generated online through interaction with the environment rather than collected in advance. SFT. We train for single epoch with global batch size of 256 and sequence length of 8192, using AdamW with learning rate of 1e5 under cosine decay and 10% warmup. Step RL. We optimize the GSPO objective, sampling 16 responses per prompt through asynchronous SGLang rollout. The policy adopts constant learning rate of 1e6 and an asymmetric clip ratio of (3e4, 4e4), and is trained for single epoch with batch size of 128 prompts. The reward follows the cascade design of Section 4.3, with the LLM-as-judge levels handled by separately served reward model. Agentic RL. We optimize the GSPO objective in an interactive Android environment that combines hundreds of parallel emulators with cluster of physical devices. Rollout is performed with asynchronous SGLang, sampling 16 responses per prompt. The policy adopts constant learning rate of 1e6 and an asymmetric clip ratio of (3e4, 4e4). The curriculum sampler scores tasks following Section 4.4, maintaining smoothed success-rate estimate with decay 0.9 and annealing α from 1.5 to 0.5 and β from 1.0 to 2.0 under temperature η = 1.0. The training batch size is 32 prompts, with maximum prompt length of 8192.

sections.title: 6.2 Grounding Results
sections.content: Grounding evaluates whether GUI agent can map natural-language target description to the correct interface element. We evaluate Xiaomi-GUI-0 on set of complementary static grounding benchmarks. ScreenSpot-V2 [48] covers standard mobile, desktop, and web grounding. MMBench-GUI-L2 [43] provides broad six-platform evaluation under basic and advanced instructions. OSWorld-G, together with its refinedinstruction variant OSWorld-G-Refine [49], targets fine-grained desktop diagnostics. Unless otherwise specified, all results use single-pass, screenshot-only setting without crop-and-zoom refinement, candidate proposal tools, self-consistency voting, or other test-time scaling, so the numbers reflect native grounding ability. Xiaomi-GUI-0 is primarily optimized for real-world Chinese mobile and in-vehicle cockpit applications, whose interfaces and interaction patterns differ substantially from those emphasized by the four public benchmarks here, which are dominated by English and desktop-style interfaces. Despite this distribution mismatch, 22 Table 4 Grounding results on four GUI grounding benchmarks. We report the overall accuracy (%) per benchmark. Per-platform and per-subcategory breakdowns are provided in Appendix (Tables 12, 13, 14, and 15).

sections.title: 6.3 Navigation Results
sections.content: Table 5 reports navigation results on the real-device benchmark RealMobile and on AndroidWorld, where Xiaomi-GUI-0-30B-A3B achieves success rate of 72.0% on RealMobile and 78.9% on AndroidWorld. Both numbers are reported as the mean over four runs to account for the relatively large evaluation variance on these benchmarks. On AndroidWorld, Xiaomi-GUI-0 attains the best result among the evaluated models, surpassing the UI-TARS, GUI-Owl, and UI-Venus series and exceeding the previously strongest UI-Venus-1.530B-A3B at 77.6%, which indicates that the proposed training pipeline strengthens long-horizon execution without sacrificing generalization on public benchmarks. On the more challenging RealMobile, Xiaomi-GUI-0 substantially outperforms locally deployable open-source models such as MAI-UI-8B at 33%, indicating that paradigms built on offline trajectories, emulators, or public benchmarks transfer poorly to real mobile conditions such as account-state changes, system dialogs, payment authentication, and risk-control interventions. Among closed-source systems, Xiaomi-GUI-0 exceeds Gemini 3.1 Flash at 58%, Claude Opus 4.7 at 60%, and Claude Opus 4.6 at 33%, and approaches frontier models such as Gemini 3.1 Pro at 85% and Seed 2.0 Pro at 80% despite their larger scale, which supports the value of real-device closed loop for narrowing the gap between benchmark scores and real-world deployability. sections.title: From Framework Agents to Native GUI Agents
sections.content: Early GUI agents are typically constructed as multi-agent orchestration systems built upon general-purpose VLMs. Methods such as the Mobile-Agent series [39, 40] and PC-Agent [19] compose specialized planner, executor, reflector, and memory modules [17] around foundation models such as GPT-4o [15], Gemini [35], or Qwen-VL [41]. The performance of these approaches is largely bounded by the underlying VLM, and inference latency grows with module depth and the number of interaction rounds. Moreover, reflection resides in the runtime procedure rather than being internalized into model weights. To overcome these limitations, recent research has increasingly shifted toward native GUI agents, in which perception, grounding, planning, and execution capabilities are integrated into single model through post-training. UI-TARS and UI-TARS2 [25, 38], AutoGLM [20], GUI-Owl in Mobile-Agent-v3 and Mobile-Agent-v3.5 [51, 54], OpenCUA [42], Step-GUI [53], UI-Venus-1.5 [36], and MAI-UI [58] all follow this paradigm. Xiaomi-GUI-0 follows this nativeagent line but adopts distinct focus: rather than optimizing for static or emulator-dominated benchmarks, it targets the constraints of real mobile deployment, including authentic account states, system dialogs, payment authentication, risk-control interventions, network variability, and client-side state dynamics. Real-Device and Virtualized GUI Benchmarks Existing mobile GUI benchmarks can be broadly divided into emulator/sandbox-based environments and real-device evaluations. The former, represented by AndroidWorld [27], constructs reproducible tasks on emulators or controlled sandboxes and verifies success through programmatic checks such as accessibility trees, UI trees, or VLM-based screenshot judges. These settings offer scalable and reproducible evaluation, but their state distributions remain biased toward simplified environments and do not fully capture the account states, page dynamics, and business logic of real applications. To improve fidelity, simulation platforms such as MobileGym [45] and SimuWoB [18] approximate the pages, databases, and partial functions of commercial applications, enabling scalable and verifiable benchmarking. They nonetheless remain distinct from their real counterparts in state distributions and client-side behaviors. Real-device benchmarks, by contrast, evaluate agents directly on physical devices and live applications. MobileBench-OL [47] measures task execution, reasoning, and noise robustness in an online real-device environment, and Knowu-bench [7] examines interactive, proactive, and personalized behaviors. Our benchmark likewise runs on physical devices and live applications, but retains rule-based programmatic evaluation for reproducible judgments and further disentangles UI navigation, grounding, planning, long-horizon execution, memory, reflection, and safety constraints, improving its diagnostic value for real-world deployment. RL-based GUI Agent Training Early works such as UI-R1 [21], GUI-R1 [22], MobileGUI-RL [33], and Mobile-R1 [11] are among the first to explore curriculum-style training and R1-style reinforcement learning for GUI agents, shifting the paradigm from purely offline imitation learning toward interactive policy optimization. DigiRL [4] demonstrates that online reinforcement learning can substantially outperform supervised fine-tuning for device-control tasks, underscoring the value of interaction feedback. Building on this foundation, subsequent studies extend the paradigm along complementary directions: self-supervised RL that removes the dependence on dense action labels [9], iterative preference learning over reasoning trajectories [14], success-rate-aware trajectory-eﬀicient policy optimization [8], process-level reward modeling for fine-grained credit assignment [50], and explicit error detection with backtracking for online recovery [46]. At scale, UI-TARS-2 [38] deploys thousands of parallel virtual machines to support large-scale RL rollouts, UI-Venus-1.5 [36] combines full-trajectory online RL with model fusion, and OpenClaw-RL [44] improves performance through asynchronous online RL. Together, these studies establish reinforcement learning as central direction for enhancing long-horizon execution, error recovery, and environmental adaptability in GUI agents.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: This report introduces Xiaomi-GUI-0, native end-to-end multimodal GUI agent for real mobile environments. Motivated by the persistent gap between high benchmark scores and real-world usability, XiaomiGUI-0 is trained and evaluated within closed loop centered on real-device execution, rather than on static benchmarks or simulated environments that only partially reflect deployment conditions. This loop couples three components. real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure treats physical devices as the primary execution environment, with sandboxes in complementary role, so that data collection, training, and evaluation share an execution distribution close to real deployment. On top of this infrastructure, an error-driven data flywheel turns the failure patterns exposed during real rollouts into supervision for reflection and recovery, rather than merely scaling up successful trajectories. progressive training recipe, proceeding from supervised fine-tuning through step-level to agentic reinforcement learning, then develops the model from basic interface operation toward long-horizon execution and error recovery. Across public grounding benchmarks, AndroidWorld, and our real-device benchmark RealMobile, Xiaomi-GUI-0 attains competitive perception and grounding and reaches 72.0% success rate on RealMobile and 78.9% on AndroidWorld, while improving end-to-end success and robustness on real devices. These results suggest that real execution is most useful not as final evaluation step but as continual source of supervision, and that treating real rollouts as the center of training and evaluation is practical path toward deployable mobile GUI agents.

sections.title: B Action Space
sections.content: Xiaomi-GUI-0 operates over compact unified action space that covers touch operations, text input, system navigation, user communication, and task termination, as summarized in Table 8. At each interaction step, the model selects exactly one action and emits its arguments as JSON object. Coordinates are expressed as relative positions in [0, 1]2, with the top-left corner at (0, 0) and the bottom-right corner at (1, 1). Table 8 Action space used by the GUI agent.

sections.title: Speak
sections.content: text Tap target coordinate, usually to select UI element or activate button. Press and hold target coordinate to open contextual menus or trigger long press interactions. Drag from one coordinate to another, mainly for scrolling, switching panels, or moving sliders. Focus an input field and enter text without automatically submitting the form. Execute search macro: focus the search field, clear existing text, enter the query, and submit. Launch target application from the system when the task requires switching or starting an application. Invoke the system back action to return to the previous page or dismiss the current layer. Return to the home screen. Wait for loading, rendering, network responses, or transient UI transitions. Ask the user for missing information, confirmation, or clarification when the task cannot proceed reliably. Stop the task with typed failure when execution is blocked by login, verification, payment authentication, unavailable results, permissions, network errors, or other predefined abnormal states. Mark non-Q&A task as completed after the requested goal has been reached. Return the final natural language answer for Q&A tasks. B.1 Anomaly Semantics As summarized in Table 9, we consolidate more than fifty abnormal-state causes observed online into fourteen handoff semantics, each corresponding to valid Fail.type value in the action space. When one of these states blocks reliable automation, the model is expected to stop, pause, or hand control back to the user rather than continue issuing UI actions. 32 Table 9 The fourteen anomaly semantics defined for the Fail.type field.

sections.title: Anomaly label
sections.content: LOGIN_REQUIRED USE_GUIDANCE CAPTCHA_VERIFICATION RESULT_NOT_FOUND BLUETOOTH_CONNECTION_REQUIRED NETWORK_ERROR PAYMENT_AUTHENTICATION TASK_CANT_FULFILLED REPEAT_OPERATION PERMISSION_REQUEST PASSWORD_REQUIRED TAKEOVER_EXIT TEMPORARY_TAKEOVER MANUAL_VERIFICATION_REQUIRED

sections.title: Trigger condition
sections.content: The current application or operation requires an account login, but the user is not logged in or the session has expired.

sections.title: Expected behavior
sections.content: Stop execution and ask the user to log in. The application is in mandatory first-use onboarding or guidance flow that cannot be skipped. Stop execution and ask the user to complete the guidance flow. The current page shows graphical captcha, slider captcha, behavior verification, or another human verification challenge. Stop execution and ask the user to complete verification. search, lookup, or filtering operation returns an empty result set, or no result satisfies the requested condition. Stop the current subtask and skip or backtrack according to the reflection strategy. The task depends on Bluetooth connection, but Bluetooth is disconnected, unavailable, or pairing fails. Stop execution and ask the user to handle the Bluetooth connection. The page cannot load normally because of connection failure, timeout, server unavailability, or another network error. Stop execution and ask the user to handle the network issue. The operation enters payment flow requiring payment password, fingerprint, face verification, SMS code, or similar authorization. Stop before payment authentication and hand control back to the user. The task itself is outside the capability of the current application or is impossible because it is ambiguous, contradictory, or unsupported. Stop the task and explain why it cannot be completed. The same action or action sequence is repeatedly executed without an effective page-state change, indicating likely loop. Stop execution to avoid meaningless repetition. The current page requests system permission such as location, camera, microphone, storage, or notifications. Stop execution and ask the user to grant or deny the permission. The page requires an account password, lock-screen password, application password, or another sensitive credential. Stop execution and let the user enter the credential. The user actively takes over the device or requests that the agent exit during execution. Immediately stop agent execution. The current step requires human intervention, but the remaining task can continue after the user finishes this step. The page requires manual secondary confirmation, such as identity verification or confirmation of sensitive operation. Pause the agent, wait for the user to finish the current step, and resume if possible. Stop execution and ask the user to confirm manually.

sections.title: C Function Tree Example
sections.content: Figure 9 presents simplified function tree for the Bilibili mobile application. For clarity, the example enumerates the sibling functions available at each level while expanding only one representative branch to the next level. This structure captures both the set of functions that can be sampled at each node and the access path from top-level application entry to concrete function point. Figure 9 Simplified function tree for the Bilibili mobile application. Each node lists the sibling functions under its parent, with single representative branch expanded to the next level. Behavior-Bucket Examples Behavior buckets provide the intent-level abstraction used during query synthesis. Whereas the function tree records which functions an application exposes and how each function is reached, behavior bucket captures how users combine those functions into coherent request. Each bucket corresponds to stable usage motivation and contains several concrete behavioral phrases. To synthesize query, the generator first samples bucket and then binds concrete entities, filters, pages, or function-tree paths to produce an executable natural-language instruction. D.1 Single-Application Example: Xiaohongshu Table 10 presents single-application behavior-bucket example for Xiaohongshu. All behaviors in this example are completed within Xiaohongshu, yet they span range of user motivations, including content consumption, local exploration, shopping, creation, social interaction, creator growth, and personal settings. sections.title: E System Prompt
sections.content: The system prompt used by Xiaomi-GUI-0 is reproduced below. At each step, the agent produces an XML-like response containing reasoning, an action description, and exactly one tool call encoded as JSON object. # Role You are GUI interaction agent. You perceive the screen, review prior steps, and decide the most , reasonable next action to fulfill the user's instruction. # Input Context 1. User instruction 2. Interaction history 3. Current device type & foreground app 4. Current screenshot submit) `<tool_call>`. scroll/move. Swipe up to scroll down) # Available Tools You MUST pick exactly one tool per step. Output the corresponding JSON string inside , 1. Tap: `{"name": "Tap", "position": [x, y], "times": 1}` (Tap at coordinate) 2. LongPress: `{"name": "LongPress", "position": [x, y]}` (Trigger contextual menus) 3. Swipe: `{"name": "Swipe", "start_position": [x1, y1], "end_position": [x2, y2]}` (Swipe to , 4. Type: `{"name": "Type", "position": [x, y], "text": "..."}` (Tap input box and type) 5. Search: `{"name": "Search", "position": [x, y], "text": "..."}` (Macro: tap -> clear -> type -> , 6. Open: `{"name": "Open", "app": "..."}` (Launch app via system) 7. Back: `{"name": "Back"}` (System-level back) 8. Home: `{"name": "Home"}` (Go to home screen) 9. Wait: `{"name": "Wait"}` (Wait for page loading/rendering) 10. Request: `{"name": "Request", "text": "..."}` (Ask user for clarification/confirmation) 11. Fail: `{"name": "Fail", "type": "...", "reason": "..."}` (Report failure. `<TYPE>` MUST be one , , , , 12. Complete: `{"name": "Complete"}` (Confirm goal reached for non-Q&A tasks) 13. Speak: `{"name": "Speak", "text": "..."}` (Present final answer for Q&A tasks) of: LOGIN_REQUIRED, USE_GUIDANCE, CAPTCHA_VERIFICATION, RESULT_NOT_FOUND, BLUETOOTH_CONNECTION_REQUIRED, NETWORK_ERROR, PAYMENT_AUTHENTICATION, TASK_CANT_FULFILLED, REPEAT_OPERATION, PERMISSION_REQUEST, PASSWORD_REQUIRED, TAKEOVER_EXIT, TEMPORARY_TAKEOVER, MANUAL_VERIFICATION_REQUIRED) Top-left is (0, 0); bottom-right is (1, 1). # Operational Constraints 1. Coordinate system: every `position` is relative [x, y] in [0, 1] with 3-decimal precision. , 2. Dismiss unrelated pop-ups (ads, upgrade prompts, rating requests) by tapping their Close / Skip , 3. Loop breaker: if three consecutive steps cause no visible change, or the same action is , , repeating in loop, self-correct (try Back or different target). If self-correction fails, call Fail. / / "Later" button rather than calling Fail. expectation. Explain what was expected vs. what is actually seen. # Reasoning Framework (inside <think>) Before emitting the action, reason inside `<think>...</think>` (omit steps if no new info): 1. [Observation]: Objectively describe the current App, page state, and key visible elements. 2. [Reflection]: (Optional) Include ONLY if the current screen deviates from the previous plan's , 3. [Plan] / [Plan Update] / [Replan]: (Choose one). Output 2-4 step path in single line , , 4. [Decision]: Deduce the exact action based on the Observation and the current `->` step in the , 5. [Memory]: Cache persistent info needed for future steps. separated by ``. Mark completed steps with `[done]` and the current step with `->`. Use [Replan] if the previous plan failed. Plan. # Output Format Your assistant message `content` MUST strictly follow this three-span XML-like shape, in this , exact order, with no extra text before, between, or after: <think> [your observation, analysis and reasoning] </think> <action> [a short natural-language description of the action, e.g., "Tap the search bar"] </action> <tool_call> {"name": "Tap", "position": [0.521, 0.123], "times": 1} </tool_call> 38 Table 12 Performance comparison of state-of-the-art models on ScreenSpot-V2.

sections.title: F Detailed Grounding Results
sections.content: This appendix complements the overall scores in Table 4 with the full per-platform and per-subcategory breakdowns for the four grounding benchmarks: ScreenSpot-V2  (Table 12)  , MMBench-GUI-L2  (Table 13)  , OSWorld-G  (Table 14)  , and OSWorld-G-Refine  (Table 15)  . These breakdowns localize the sources of performance differences across platforms, instruction diﬀiculty levels, and diagnostic capability dimensions that single averaged score obscures. Specifically, ScreenSpot-V2 reports text and icon grounding across mobile, desktop, and web, while MMBench-GUI-L2 reports platform-specific accuracy under basic and advanced instructions. OSWorld-G together with OSWorld-G-Refine further characterize the model along text matching, element recognition, layout understanding, fine-grained manipulation, and refusal.

sections.title: G Case Study
sections.content: To illustrate the behavior of Xiaomi-GUI-0 during real-device execution, we present two qualitative case studies on complementary aspects of the agent: end-to-end task completion and mid-trajectory error recovery. The first case, shown in Figure 10, traces complete execution process. Given user instruction, the agent observes the current screen, decomposes the task into intermediate sub-goals, and emits sequence of GUI actions until the goal is reached. The trajectory demonstrates that the unified action space supports the full pipeline from intent understanding to task completion on physical device. The second case, shown in Figure 11, illustrates the agents reflection and self-correction behavior. When the observed screen state deviates from the expected outcome of the preceding action, the agent records the discrepancy in its reflection field, revises its plan, and selects corrective action rather than continuing the original trajectory. This behavior shows how explicit reflection enables the agent to recover from intermediate uncertainty during interaction. 39 Figure 10 Case study of complete real-device execution trajectory, from the initial user instruction to task completion. Figure 11 Case study of reflection and plan revision during real-device interaction, where the agent detects deviation from its expected state and recovers with corrective action. 41 Table 13 Performance comparison of state-of-the-art models on MMBench-GUI-L2.
